concise_definition: Corner : The corner typically consists of a certain area on either side of the court that is in the general vicinity of a sideline and its adjoining baseline.

sections.heading: What is the corner in basketball?
sections.text:
The corner typically consists of a certain area on either side of the court that is in the general vicinity of a sideline and its adjoining baseline.

The simple example below shows the corner areas denoted with text on a basketball diagram.

sections.heading: Basic diagram of corner areas
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of corner areas denoted with text.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of corner areas denoted with text.]

Generally speaking, the corner areas could be located by starting at the bottom of the rim in the center of the lane area and extending an imaginary line out towards the sideline in either direction.

Also, the corner areas are situated behind the three-point line and as stated earlier, generally cover the intersection point between the sideline and thebaseline.

sections.heading: What is the short corner?
sections.text:
The short corners comprise the general areas inside of the three-point line that are between the corner areas and the basket.

The short corners are also slightly below the low post areas and are adjacent to the baseline.

The short corner areas are typically utilized within certain strategies such as zone offense to help the team create scoring opportunities against the defense.

Moreover, the short corner could be considered a sub-type or secondary variant when compared to the primary corner area that is generally established behind the three-point line.

The diagram below demonstrates the short corners alongside other notable areas on the court labeled with text.

[Image Alt Text: Example diagram of short corners plus additional areas labeled with text.]

[Image Alt Text: Example diagram of short corners plus additional areas labeled with text.]

As mentioned previously, the corner is near the sideline and the adjoining baseline while the short corner is between the basket and corner inside of the three-point line.

The top is the general area located behind the three-point line in the middle of the offensive team’s frontcourt.

Theslotsconsist of areas established between the top as well as between the wings that are adjacent to the sidelines.

Thewingsconsist of the court areas that are adjacent to the nearest sideline generally at or above the free throw line extended, which is an imaginary line that runs from the edges of the free throw line to the sidelines.

Thehigh postis the general area that covers the free throw line from one edge to the other as well as the adjacent area that is between the free throw line and the top of the three-point line. What’s more, the edges of the free throw line are typically known as the high post elbows or simply, the elbow areas of the court.

Thelow postareas are slightly above the basket and just outside of the lane lines, which are borders for the lane. Additionally, the lane area, also referred to as the key, comprises the area that is between the low posts and below the free throw line.

sections.heading: What are additional insights about the corner in relation to defense?
sections.text:
It should be mentioned that a corner could technically be indicated by a sideline and its adjoining half court line, particularly in reference totrap defenseas well as certain types of press defense.

That is sometimes referred to as acoffin corner, which would be a corner area of the court that an offensive player in possession of the ball would have difficulty escaping from, specifically if that same offensive player gets trapped in that spot by two defenders.

The diagram below demonstrates this concept.

sections.heading: Coffin corner example: Part 1
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An that demonstrates an offensive player dribbling towards a coffin corner.]

[Image Alt Text: An that demonstrates an offensive player dribbling towards a coffin corner.]

This is an example of a1-1-3 press defensethat could result in an offensive player entering into a coffin corner area of the court.

In terms of the overall action, 1 receives the ball via aninbound passfrom 4, indicated by the dotted arrow.

Following that, the general idea is for X1 to influence 1 towards the nearest sideline. Afterwards, 1 dribbles down the sideline, represented by the wavy arrow, while most of the other offensive and defensive players move down the court accordingly.

Also, to keep things simple, let’s assume that 1 is unaware that a trap is being set by the opposing team and continues to dribble down the sideline and eventually into the offensive team’sfrontcourtarea.

sections.heading: Coffin corner example: Part 2
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example that demonstrates how an offensive player could get trapped within a coffin corner.]

[Image Alt Text: An example that demonstrates how an offensive player could get trapped within a coffin corner.]

Again, let’s assume that 1 dribbles into the frontcourt and immediately gets trapped by X1 and X4.

In this instance, 1 has essentially entered into what could be considered a coffin corner. This is because 1 cannot easily release defensive pressure, particularly with a backpedalingescape dribbleas that would typically result in abackcourt violation.

Furthermore, 1 cannot easily throw a pass to either 3 or 4 as those offensive players are beingdeniedor at least pressured by X3 and X2.

Even if 1 attempts to throw askip passacross the court towards 2, it is possible that X5 could sprint to intercept that pass provided they have thespeedto do so while X3 drops back to protect the basket.

Moreover, even if 5 does not try for an interception, the general longer distance of the pass and its air time gives the defense enough time to recover towards the ball or potentially convert into another type of defense such asman to manorzone defense.

The overall point though is that once 1 gets trapped in the corner area near the half court line and sideline, again sometimes referred to as a coffin corner, that limits their offensive options in terms ofdribblingorpassing.
